The semi rushes out from under an overpass and we WATCH it until it gets small on the southern horizon and all we are LOOKING AT is sky. FEMALE VOICE (V.O.) There's this missionary driving through China and he sees this dog in one of the fields with a wooden leg. Our VIEW MOVES DOWN TO:
EXT. COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY - NEW YORK CITY - DAY
The campus is crowded. Ally (now 19 and played by the same actress who played Annie) is walking TOWARD US. She carries a binder and books and is accompanied by three friends -PAT, KARIN, and LYNN.
So he stops and asks the farmer, `Why does your dog have a wooden leg?' The farmer says...
The girls start to enter an intersection on the walkway. TOM DONNELLY, 21 -- shaggy hair, attractive, granny glasses, coming in from a side path -- calls to them.
Excuse me. Can you help me? I'm looking for Larry Pulsky's class... It's a production class... It's in the film department.
You're not even close.
Tom's gaze brushes over Ally, who's staring at him. She's already beginning to hear the echoes.
It's behind the library.
I have no idea where the library is.
You're new, aren't you?
Of course he's new. Didn't you just hear him say he didn't know where the library was?
Go back this way to the student union, go left, you'll pass a brick building on your right, go right, just past it cut through the quad, you'll be
in front of the library, the film deparment is in the building behind
TOM CONCENTRATES, THEN:
Thank you.
He starts off in the direction she's indicated. He hasn't gone six feet when he turns around.
I'm sorry. This is very embarrassing. Could you go through that again? I'm totally lost.
Don't worry. It happens to all of us.
I'll take you over there. I'm going in that direction anyway. I'll see you guys later.
(as Ally and Tom walk off) I thought you had English Lit next.
No.
I hope this isn't too far out of your way.
It is... but you're new. I'm Ally
Chandler.
(takes her hand) Tom Donnelly.
Their fingers slip slowly apart. A little further on, Ally sneaks a look at Tom. She's met someone this way before and he was wonderful. Tom is looking straight ahead.
INT. OFFICE - BOSTON PUBLISHING HOUSE - DAY

Elmo, dressed in a cheap tie and sweater, is waiting across a large mahogany desk from MR. TURNER. The afternoon sun that almost silhouettes the senior editor washes across Elmo's face, exposing his innocence and his hope. Mr. Turner puts down Elmo's application. He takes off his bifocals and spins them while he thinks. They flash like mirrors when hit by the light.
(kind) I'm very intrigued, Mr. Barnett. You're a high school dropout. Why did you apply for a job in publishing? It's all books.
(with his stutter) I've always liked to read. I thought maybe there was something I could do with that.
That's very well said. Now why should I hire you?
(pause, then:) Because I need a future and I'm willing to work hard for it.
Do you have any idea what kind of job you'd want?
I don't even know what kind of jobs you have. But I'll do anything to start. I'm not dumb, Mr. Turner. I'm only 20. I can learn. And you can get me cheap.
You're very tempting, Mr. Barnett. You'd certainly bring a different point of view to things.
That wouldn't necessarily be bad.
Give me a chance.
I see you're not married. Are you engaged?
No... I guess I haven't found the right girl yet.
Mr. Turner spins his glasses and thinks.
I'll tell you my biggest fear. I think you could do it... but I don't know if you'd stick with it. I just feel that one day you'd up and take off and where would that leave me?
(looking at the desk) I understand.
(genuine) I'm sorry, Mr. Barnett.
Elmo rises with Mr. Turner and the two men shake hands.
I understand. Thanks a lot for seeing me. You've been very nice.
Thank you.
He remains standing as Elmo leaves.
Mr. Barnett...?
Elmo looks back from the doorway.
Please don't give up.
Not me. I'm gonna do great.

with palm trees and blue water. As we PULL BACK, we FIND Elmo in a corporal's uniform. He is 23 years old and his hair is short. As we PULL FURTHER BACK, we REALIZE that the sandy beach is on a travel poster advertising the Virgin Islands and that Elmo is standing in front of a "ride shelter" outside the FORT LEONARD WOOD ARMY BASE in KANSAS. He has his thumb out to the traffic and his collar up against the wind. It's SNOWING LIGHTLY. The COWBOY SONG about "lost love" that was playing on the pickup radio when Mike asked Brenda to go to California FADES UP ON THE SOUNDTRACK. A new (1972) Ford Fairlane slows to a stop and the passenger door is thrown open. Elmo looks in at a burly, red-headed "feed" SALESMAN.
Where do you want to go, Corporal?
Wherever you are?
Hop in.
As Elmo throws his travel bag in the back, the Salesman notices the Silver Star medal on his chest.
Where you get that?
Hue.
Holy Jesus Christ, boy, you're lucky to be alive.
The Fairlane pulls back onto the highway.
INT. FORD FAIRLANE - NIGHT

The Salesman is smoking a cigar and watching the headlights of the oncoming traffic. The COWBOY SONG is NOW COMING FROM the RADIO. Elmo turns from a reflection of himself in the windshield and stares out at the darkened landscape. The
INT. ST. PATRICK'S CATHEDRAL -- DAY

It's decorated for a wedding and half filled with well-dressed FRIENDS and RELATIVES. At the altar, Ben looks on with pride and a tear in his eye as Ally, dazzlingly beautiful in her wedding gown, stands with Tom before the MONSIGNOR. The COWBOY SONG ENDS and the Monsignor turns to Ally.
Do you, Ally Chandler, take Tom Donnelly to be your lawful wedded husband, to love and to hold in sickness or in health, till death do you part?
I do.
EXT. CHANDLER ESTATE -- GREAT NECK -- LONG ISLAND -- DAY

A BAND LEADER gives a downbeat and his MUSICIANS slip into "The Tennessee Waltz". The wedding guests applaud as Tom leads Ally to the middle of a parquet floor set up on the lawn. He takes her in his arms and, with everyone watching, they begin to dance. A middle-aged MAN in an Air Force uniform sidles up to Ben and whispers in his ear.
Bet this spread set you back a couple hula hoops, Sarge.
(twists around and greets the Man warmly) Hey, Andy, glad you could make it.
She's beautiful, Ben. I'd never know she was your daughter.
They turn back to the dance floor.
He makes movies, doesn't he?
Right now he's parking cars for a living.
A light breeze billows the catering tent behind them.
Look at them... isn't it great?
They're in love and they've got the whole world ahead of them.
I just hope he loves her as much as
she loves him. On the floor, the couple splits and, as Tom goes over to get his mother, Ally comes over to Ben.
Come on, Dad, it's your dance.
Ben is very happy to take his daughter in his arms and she's very happy to be there. She lays her head on his shoulder and we WATCH them for a long time as they glide around the floor.
EXT. SIDEWALK -- MIDWESTERN TOWN -- DAY
We TAKE A LONG LOOK AT the pulsing, wobbling flow of PEDESTRIAN traffic. Our VIEW at first IS RANDOM, then it PICKS OUT a well-endowed WOMAN in a halter top and tight jeans.
Look at this... look at 'em bounce... Look at 'em... God, I love to watch
tits bounce. I love to feel them. I

A box-back truck with "Hot & Cold Insulation" emblazoned on its side sits at the curb. Elmo and his co-worker, SHORTY (the name says it all), are lunching and girl watching in their coveralls from the open bay door.
(scratching under the goggles hanging from his neck) Check out this guy.
A man wearing an abusively ugly hat goes by them.
It's got to be his lucky hat. Elmo sneezes sharply.
Bless you.
He cocks his head. There's the FAINT SOUND OF HEAVEN around them.
I just got a great idea. Garbage cans that repel ants. What do you think?

What do you mean? Just that. Garbage cans that repel ants. You know when you go out at night and you feel around to stuff the garbage bag in the can, always when you pull your hands out you have ants crawling all over your arms. An hour later you're still picking them out of your hair.
Elmo is looking so intently at him that it makes Shortly nervous.
You think I'm crazy but it's true. Who do you know who likes ants? Nobody. It's a good idea, Elmo.
I think it's great.
Really?
Absolutely. It's wonderful. It's never been done.
Oh my God!... Look at this. Look at this.
Coming along the sidewalk is a beautiful young WOMAN. A vision. An angel. She seems to float while the rest of the pedestrians walk.
It's a great idea, Shorty. We've got to do something with it.
We will. She's gorgeous. She wins today.
We just WATCH the girl as she continues TOWARD US.
You ever wonder who they are, Shorty? Where girls like that are from? Who are their friends? Where do they go
at night? Who do they marry?
Miss...
The vision turns toward them.
Oh geez, Shorty. Come on.
Slightly wary, the girl approaches. Elmo is looking for a place to hide.
My friend here wants to ask you a question.
Elmo just stares at her.
It's your dime, Elmo.
(stutters) Hi.
Hi.
We HEAR A CAR HONK O.S.
INT. ALLY'S OFFICE - LATE AFTERNOON

Our VIEW PANS OVER a drawing of a child's slipper made in the shape of a red Corvette. A FRONT VIEW SHOWS it with the headlights on. Ally is bent over the drawing putting the black on the tires with a flow-tip pen. BZZZZZ! She jumps and fumbles off the ALARM ON HER WRISTWATCH.
INT. CHANDLER TOY CO. -- NEW YORK CITY -- LATE AFTERNOON

Ally comes out of her office. She's in a hurry and struggling with a stylish coat, a shoulder bag, and a sheaf of drawings. We FOLLOW her DOWN the hallway THROUGH a reception area that displays some of the toys the Chandler Co. makes -- the hula hoop, a skiing penguin, a pair of clacking dentures -- TO her father's office. She taps and, without waiting for an answer, enters.
INT. BEN'S OFFICE -- LATE AFTERNOON
At the work table Ben is going over the blueprints for a new toy with a couple of ENGINEERS.
(interrupting) These are the footlight things I was telling you about.
She gives her father the drawings, kisses him on the cheek, and heads for the door.
Wait, wait... (on the way to his desk) I finished reading this last night.
He picks a manuscript off his desk and hands it to Ally.
I didn't know you remembered Mike.
Did you like it?
A lot. What are you gonna do with it?

(blushes) I don't know. Maybe try and get it published... something... you know... (recovers) The batteries are going to be the problem with the footlights. There's nothing on the market right now that's small enough. I'll see you in the morning.
EXT. ELEGANT RESTAURANT -- LONG ISLAND -- NIGHT

It's busy. Cars are lined up on the street. Ally, in a windbreaker and jeans, scribbles a number on a claim check and hands it to a man getting out of a Jaguar. Another ATTENDANT jumps behind the wheel of the Jaguar and takes off. A silver Mercedes moves up. Tom backs a yellow Corvette down in front of it and, while he holds the door for his PATRONS, the COUPLE getting out of the Mercedes catches his eye. Ally hands the long-haired man a claim check. Tom gets his tip and trots over to the stand. Ally gives him a flying kiss.
I love you.
(looking at the longhaired man) That's Nort Thorton.
Who's Nort Thorton?
He's a terrific commercial director.
Go talk to him.
You crazy?
Why not?
Because I'll lose my job if I start doing that.
So what? This isn't the job you want. That's the job you want.
A CAR in line HONKS.
(starts towards the Mercedes) He wouldn't be interested, anyway.
(following) You don't know that unless you talk to him.
Don't push it, Ally.
But you're good. You're not asking for a favor. You're good.
He's a commercial director. I want to do features.
It would be a place to start.
You better get that car.
Ally just stands there and watches Tom gun the Mercedes up the ramp.
EXT. PARKING LOT -- GOODYEAR TIRE AND RUBBER PLANT -- LUNCH HOUR

Elmo and Shorty get out of the "Hot & Cold Insulation" truck and start toward the building. Shorty's borrowed sports coat is sizes too big for him and Elmo's is just the wrong side of tight. Both men are very nervous.
All we've got to remember is that it's a great idea and there's nothing like it on the market.
Okay, okay. (points) Nice car.
What?... Oh, yeah. (looks at Shorty again) I think your tie goes better with what I'm wearing and my tie goes better with what you're wearing.
Okay, okay.
Without stopping the men remove their ties, exchange them, and begin to put them on.
(during the exchange) Remember, we want ten thousand dollars apiece.
Okay, okay.
And ten percent of the profits and we want to be involved.
Okay, okay.
They walk on.
What if they offer us eight percent?
Then we'll negotiate.
Elmo and Shorty enter the building.
INT. OFFICE -- GOODYEAR TIRE AND RUBBER PLANT -- DAY

A lean, gray-haired MAN with dark-rimmed glasses and a large Adam's apple is rocking back and forth in his chair howling with laughter while Elmo and Shorty stare at him in stunned silence. Weakly, he reaches out and jabs the intercom.
Burt, you gotta get in here and listen to what these two guys came up with. Hurry.
EXT. PARKING LOT -- GOODYEAR TIRE AND RUBBER PLANT -- DAY
His jaw set against the world, Elmo strides angrily back toward the car. Shorty trots anxiously alongside.
He's an asshole. Forget it. We'll take it someplace else.
Elmo rips the tie from around his neck and throws it into the cars. Shorty's eyes follow it. That's his tie! But Elmo is right.
(whipping the tie from around his neck) They're assholes. All of 'em. Assholes. (turns and throws the tie at the building) Assholes!!
He starts to catch up with Elmo, but his anger is still not satisfied and he whirls around to face the building again.
ASSHOLES!!!!
INT. OFFICES -- HOT & COLD INSULATION -- LATE AFTERNOON
Nervously twirling his cap, Elmo, now in his coveralls, waits on the edge of the bench in the reception area. He looks at the SECRETARY. Her face is blank.
You know what he wants me for? Was it because Shorty and I were late after lunch?
She shrugs. Elmo shuffles his feet. At the end of the hallway, a man with a crew cut, MR. MULVEHILL, sticks his head out of an office.
Elmo...
He waits for Elmo to come to him, then without a word ushers him into the office and closes the door.
INT. OFFICE -- LATE AFTERNOON
Sit down. (watches Elmo do as he's told) Elmo, I want you to be my foreman.
Elmo just looks at him.
You can smile. This is a promotion.
INT. ONE-BEDROOM APARTMENT -- DAWN

A crack of light from the hallway falls across a nurse's cap on a dresser littered with a collection of dolls. In the bed, the girl Elmo and Shorty thought was so beautiful on the street lies asleep in a tangle of sheets. Elmo is watching her from a chair next to the door. He looks sad. Finally, he rubs his eyes and, clutching a handful of shirts, stands up.
IN THE BATHROOM
he takes a razor, toothbrush, aspirin, and a nail clipper out of the medicine chest and tosses them into his shaving kit.
IN THE KITCHEN

with the cold gray light of dawn seeping in the windows, he stuffs the shirts, shaving kit, and a stack of paperbacks into his travel bag, then shrugs into a jacket. The house key clinks softly as he lays it on the counter. He turns to pick up his travel bag. The girl is standing in the archway. There are tears in her eyes.
Is it me? Did I do something wrong? Elmo looks at his shoe tops.
What do you want, Elmo? What's the matter?
Nothing.
EXT. CARPORT -- DAWN

His head down, Elmo walks slowly across the back yard to a '59 Impala convertible that was once white. He gets to the car, turns around, and heads back to the apartment building. Halfway there he stops, turns around, and goes back to the car. He stands quietly next to it thinking, then explodes. The travel bag is slammed against the car, his fists flail at the wall of the carport, his feet dent the door of the car. All this is done without uttering a sound and then it's over. He stands there panting, exhausted.
EXT. HIGHWAY -- DAWN

The '59 Impala is cruising along with Elmo behind the wheel. On the RADIO there's a NEWS ITEM ABOUT NIXON'S RESIGNATION. FROM WAY ABOVE the highway, we WATCH as the Chevy comes to a four-way intersection in the middle of nowhere. There is no other traffic. The Chevy starts straight across, then changes its mind and goes left. But that's not right and it goes left again, making a circle in the intersection. Finally, it decides on the only direction left to it and it clatters off, trailing a column of smoke. We BEGIN TO HEAR THE SOUND OF APPLAUSE.
INT. BALLROOM -- CLIO AWARDS -- NIGHT
On stage the PRESENTER waits for the applause to die down. PRESENTER And now the winner for best comedy commercial of the year...

Paul Esposito, Director Tom Donnelly. At their table Ally and Tom kiss and embrace and, while Ally claps proudly, Tom and a good-looking older MAN bound on stage and accept their Clio's. Arm in arm they hold them up to the applause of the audience.
EXT. HOTEL -- NEW YORK -- NIGHT

The show is over and the black tie CROWD is drifting out. Ally and Tom are among those waiting for their limousines. Ally has both arms around Tom's waist and her head on his chest. His tie is undone and, with the Clio in one hand, he has both arms around her.
How would you feel about us having a baby?
(wary) Are you pregnant?
No... but I've been thinking about it. That could be our thing for next year. You'll make a movie and I'll have a baby.
(forcing enthusiasm) Eh... yeah.
The GIRL in a couple about their age looks at them and whispers to her COMPANION. He nods.
Thank you. Thank you very much.
Tom looks on with a vague disquiet.
I didn't have a pretend friend. But when Mike had to leave, I cried. It was great. (afterthought to Tom) Congratulations.
Thank you.
The couples go back to ignoring each other.
INT. LIMOUSINE - NEW YORK - NIGHT
Ally is snuggled up to Tom.
I'm glad you were lost at school.
(after too long a pause) So am I.
EXT. MAIN STREET - MIDWESTERN TOWN - EVENING

The people are flowing TOWARD US. We ARE ELMO'S EYES. Even though he doesn't know it, he is LOOKING FOR Annie. YOUNG COUPLES, LOVERS, WOMEN, and GIRLS come under closer scrutiny than anyone else. Some of the returned glances are interested, but they're not right and Elmo passes them by. As he stands at a street corner waiting for a light, a large digital clock face in a store behind him flips off the seconds -- 58, 59, 60, ending the minute, changing the hour. Elmo starts across the street. He's beginning to realize he has a deadline.
INT./EXT. WOODEN SHED - DOCK AREA - CHESAPEAKE BAY AFTERNOON

The needle on an upright scale swings across the numbers. A CHECKER in a white smock registers the weight and nods. Elmo lifts a crate of live hard-shell crabs off the scale plate and starts across the cement. The crabs reach through the slats with their claws and snap at his eyes. There are crabs everywhere. They scuttle underfoot. One of them has attached itself to Elmo's pant leg. He tries to kick it off as he goes to drop the crate into the back of a pickup parked in the doorway. He spends the afternoon lifting these crates off stacks and onto scales and into the backs of a succession of pickups. At the end of the shift, his BOSS counts two crisp ten-dollar bills into Elmo's outstretched hand.
There you be.
INT./EXT. '59 IMPALA CONVERTIBLE - MORNING

Elmo is driving with one hand. The other runs the TUNER THROUGH THE RADIO BANDS searching for something to listen to. We CATCH A BIT OF A NEWS ITEM on the CAPTURE OF PATTY HEARST before he SETTLES ON A good old-fashioned ROCK AND ROLL FOOT STOMPER, Frankie Ford's "SEA CRUISE".
INT. ALLY AND TOM'S APARTMENT - NEW YORK - MORNING

It's big and sparsely but beautifully furnished. The RADIO is up so LOUD that "SEA CRUISE" is ricocheting off the walls. Ally, wearing nothing but a pair of bikini panties, is all alone and having the time of her life dancing to the music. She ponys around the couch to the breakfast bar to pick up a piece of toast. Without missing a beat she twirls around and takes a bite.
INT./EXT. '59 IMPALA CONVERTIBLE - MORNING

"SEA CRUISE" ROCKS ON. Elmo is slapping the steering wheel in rhythm. A six pack of Kellogg's breakfast cereals is wedged into the seat next to him. He reaches down and thumbs open a Sugar Pops and shakes some into his mouth.
INT. ALLY AND TOM'S NEW YORK APARTMENT - MORNING

Ally takes a sip of orange juice and pretends that this was just an interruption in her performance of "SEA CRUISE". She joins Frankie, making the song a duet as she struts her stuff across the living room.
INT./EXT. '59 IMPALA CONVERTIBLE - MORNING
Elmo is rocking out, bouncing up and down in his seat while he sings along with Frankie.
INT. ALLY AND TOM'S NEW YORK APARTMENT - MORNING
Ally is dancing. The PHONE RINGS. It RINGS AGAIN. She TURNS OFF the RADIO and, in the quiet, walks over and picks up the receiver.
Hello... Oh, hi love...
"SEA CRUISE" JUMPS UP and bites US in the ass.
INT./EXT. '59 IMPALA CONVERTIBLE - MORNING

At the top of his lungs Elmo blazes through the finale. CRASH!!!! It could have been the music, it could have been the pounding on the dash, or just old age. But whatever it was, the Chevy's transmission has fallen out onto the highway along with a lot of other parts.
EXT. SERVICE BAY - GAS STATION - AFTERNOON
A dejected Elmo is looking at his dead car.
(standing next to him) Let's see here. Parts, labor... we're looking at five fifty.
Elmo takes a long look at the Impala.
How much will you give me for it?
What would I want with this piece of shit?
INT. TRUCK CAB - NIGHT
Elmo, sporting a black eye and a split lip, climbs in with his travel bag and a paperback. The DRIVER, a skinny blond man with a pockmarked face, moves his rig back out onto the highway.
Where you headed?
Wherever you are, pal.
EXT. BIG SKY COUNTRY - LATE AFTERNOON
Elmo is a mere dot, trudging along the side of the road. Behind him the clouds hang low over a beautiful valley. It is misting and Elmo is thinking.
EXT. COUNTRY HIGHWAY - LATE AFTERNOON 125

A car comes along and Elmo sticks out his thumb. The car doesn't stop, but as Elmo swivels to follow its progress, he sees a man sitting on a rock next to the road up ahead. That's strange. Elmo can't remember him being there before. He switches his travel bag from one shoulder to the other and continues. As Elmo gets closer, the man on the rock takes a drag on his cigarette and stands up. He is wearing old blue coveralls and a flannel shirt. Emmett Humbird is waiting for Elmo.
Happy birthday, Elmo.

Elmo stops. He has no idea who this man is. Emmett takes a fresh cigarette out of his breast pocket and suddenly, FROM ELMO'S POV, EVERYTHING SLOWS DOWN. He WATCHES INTENTLY as Emmett lights the new cigarette off the butt of the old one. The glowing tip presses into the new tobacco. The paper sizzles and begins to burn. ZAP! Echoes from Heaven whisper through Elmo's mind.
Emmett Humbird, Mike. Glad to meet you. (A little fainter) Emmett Humbird, Mike. Glad to meet you.
Things RETURN TO NORMAL SPEED... ALMOST.
Thanks to you I can only smoke down here.
Am I supposed to know you?
(after a long drag) We met in Heaven.
(crosses the road) Why don't you take the next car? I'll walk on this side for a while.
You think you're looking for her, but you're just drifting.
I'm not the only one who's drifting, pal.
That promotion at the insulation company... it wasn't the trap you thought it was. Shorty's idea about the garbage cans, you should have stuck with that, you could have made it something.
Elmo is starting to really get scared and picks up his pace. In the distance we can SEE a white van coming along the road TOWARD US.
(paralleling him) You asked for this life, Elmo. You asked to be born.
(over his shoulder) I don't know what you're talking about, man. I don't know what you're talking about.
I know things have been hard for you but you better start believing in yourself or you're going to miss her. And if you miss her this time you'll never find her again.
Elmo whirls to point a finger at Emmett.
Listen, asshole, if you don't stop following me, I'm gonna come over there and break you in half.
Did you notice you're not stuttering?
Elmo puts a hand to his mouth. He realizes he hasn't been.
Remember Heaven, Elmo. Just think of where you want to be and you're there... (points to a spot in front of him) Come here.
Elmo takes a step back and stumbles into a run.
Oh damn...
He snaps his fingers. In midstride, Elmo is jerked off the ground and strobed back across the road to face Emmett.
His knees give way and Emmett grabs his arms to steady him.
Listen to me. You're 27 today. You only have three more years. So you better get moving.
The van stops opposite them.
(softly) I've got to go now... It's all very simple, Elmo. Just imagine and believe in yourself.
He lets go of Elmo's arms and walks backward to the van.
I care about you, Elmo. (smiles) I want you to get the girl.

He disappears behind the van and, without hearing a door open or close, it immediately speeds off. As Elmo watches it go, the clouds above break and a shaft of sunlight falls across the highway. The van hits it and VANISHES. Elmo's amazement is cut short by the SOUND OF A VOICE behind him.
Hey, you want a ride or don't you?
Elmo looks back. An old Plymouth is waiting for him on the shoulder. The DRIVER is standing next to it.
(stuttering again) Did you just see a white van go by?
I haven't seen a car for miles. That's why I stopped for you. Do you want a ride?
After a last glance at the shaft of light, Elmo runs to the car.
INT. PLYMOUTH - LATE AFTERNOON
It is moving.
Where you headed?
(mumbles) I don't know.
EXT. COUNTRYSIDE - LATE AFTERNOON
The Plymouth is the only car in sight.
INT. OFFICE BUILDING - NEW YORK - EVENING

Ally is leaning against the wall in a hallway. She's frail and drawn and struggling to hold herself together. The SOUNDS OF MONDAY NIGHT FOOTBALL are seeping out of a nearby office. Two older MEN pass her locked in their own conversation. They're nothing to Ally. Her face brightens when the door she's been watching opens and a group of advertising MEN files out and moves toward the elevators. Tom is among them.
Tom...
He looks annoyed to see her.
(to the Men) Excuse me.
He crosses to Ally.
I just came over to give you this.
She holds out the pink toy mouse we SAW in Heaven.
It's our Christmas thing. It was my
idea. It does a back flip. Tom reluctantly takes the gift and looks toward the Men waiting for him.
It's nice.
Pause.
I must look terrible. I haven't been sleeping too much.
I'm sorry.
I love you.
Tom doesn't answer. One of the Men calls from the elevators.
We'll see you downstairs, Tom.
What are you working on?
I'm doing a bunch of spots for the Jimmy Carter campaign.
Oh, that's great.
I gotta go.
I know it's over between us, but... eh... so I'll know what to do in the future... what did I do wrong?
You didn't do anything wrong.
Then why don't you love me?
Ally, you're a wonderful girl. There's
going to be a lot of other guys. Ally fights back the tears.
But I want you.
Oh please, Ally, don't cry...
Are we going to be able to see each other?
I love you so much.
Tom leans in and kisses her on the cheek.
I gotta go. Take care of yourself.
He turns away and then turns back; there are tears in his eyes.
You always thought I could do anything. I can't. You pushed too hard. You expected too much of me.
But I didn't expect anything that wasn't there.
Jesus, Ally...
I'll change. Really, Tom, I promise I'll change.

Listen to me, Ally. I'm not telling you to change. I'm telling you I'm tired of trying to keep up with you. (he's crying now) Maybe it's because you work in toys. I don't know. You're like a kid. You don't think anything's impossible. You get an idea and just go ahead and do it. That's not the way it is with most people. And it's goddamned hard to live with.

He and Ally just stand there crying. There's nothing more to say. Tom wipes a hand over his eyes and leaves her. Ally tries to shield her tears from the people passing by. CHEERS FLOAT IN FROM THE MONDAY NIGHT FOOTBALL.
INT. LOCKER ROOM - TRUCK STOP - NIGHT

Elmo is standing in a shower stall with the WATER POUNDING against his neck. His head is down and he is lost in himself. There are other stalls in use, but ALL SOUND FADES AS WE MOVE IN ON Elmo. A smile flickers across his face. He's made a decision and likes it. He raises his head and looks DIRECTLY INTO THE CAMERA and we HOLD ON him FOR A LONG MOMENT.
INT. POLICE STATION - NEW YORK - NIGHT

Concern etched on his face, Ben Chandler waits at the front desk. It's a busy night and he looks out of place in his tuxedo. The door leading to the cells opens and Ben's heart goes into his throat. Ally stumbles out on the arm of a MATRON. She's ridiculously drunk. Mascara streaks her face and there's vomit down her front. Just before Ben reaches her, she slips out of the Matron's grasp and lands with a thump on her ass. Some people in the crowd laugh. One MAN claps. Ally begins to cry. Ben kneels down next to her and puts his arms around her.
Shh... shh... it's okay, Ally. It's okay...
Daddy...
Everything's going to be okay...
Daddy...
Let's go. Come on. Upsy daisy...
He gently raises her to her feet and tries to steady her.
You need any help?
We're gonna be fine. Thank you. Let's go, baby...
He tries to walk Ally out, but her legs are gone and she sags against him.
Why doesn't he love me, Daddy?
He kisses her on the cheek, lifts her into his arms, and carries her out of the station.
EXT. BEACH - LONG ISLAND - DAY
It's early winter and gray. A four-year-old BOY with a Great Dane on a leash is walking AWAY FROM US. The dog is a little taller than the boy.
Why don't you get away from here for a while? Go out to Los Angeles and take over the footlights project. Nobody can figure out how to make them work. Maybe you can.
Ally and her father ENTER OUR VIEW. They're also walking AWAY FROM US.
I don't want to do that anymore. I'm tired of working in toys. I feel stupid. I want to grow up.
You don't think I'm grown up?
You know what I mean.

If by growing up you mean that you would abandon your talent to create toys that make children smile, which in turn make their parents happy, and when they go to work they do something nice for someone else and the world is a better place for it, then I would look real hard at growing up.
The SOUND OF THE WAVES fills their silence.
How can I get him back?
You can't... You did a lot for Tom. He'll be fine... I'll make you a deal. Give me a year. If at the end of that you feel the same way about toys, then we'll sell the company.
That's not what I'm saying.
I know that. But I built it for you. And if you don't want it, then it's not going to be the same.
Ally and her father LEAVE OUR VIEW.
Dad, that's not fair.
Don't worry about that. What do you say? ALLY (O.S.) I'll give you a year.
The little boy and the dog continue on their way.
EXT. DESERT HIGHWAY - DAY

Elmo is standing on the shoulder holding up a sign that reads "California" to the oncoming traffic. His eyes follow the passing cars and then go back to an open paperback in his left hand. A new ('76) Ford Grenada passes him and begins to pull over. It stops so far down the road Elmo isn't sure it's for him until it starts to back up. He grabs his travel bag and runs to meet it. There is an older COUPLE in the front seat. Elmo opens the back door.
Thanks a lot... The Man and Woman smiling at him as he wriggles in are the parents Elmo had when he was Mike. They are a little older but easily RECOGNIZABLE.
INT. FORD GRENADA - DAY
It is back in the flow of traffic.
How far are you folks going?
We'll be able to take you to California. We're going to Los Angleeze.
Oh, that's great... that's great.
You live in Los Angle-eze?
No... I... eh... I'm from Maine.
You're a long way from home.
So are we, Steve.
Where you from?
You probably never heard of it. Sunbury, Pennsylvania... north of Harrisburg on the Susquehanna River.
Elmo nods. Everybody nods. Nobody knows what to say. Finally:

(pause) We had a son... Mike... Mikey... He was about your age and... like you he was going to California... without a job... He got killed about a hundred miles from home. Kind of. Every Christmas we get cards from the two children. The little girl is married and has children of her own and the little boy is a doctor in Boston. Poor you... all you wanted was a ride to California and you get a crazy old lady who probably has scared the dickens out of you.

Donald and Ally are walking along the hallway in silence. Ally is wrapped tightly in herself and her mind is racing ahead to the moment at the door. ... the plant in Taiwan, the plant in Mexico, the plant and research center in California, the offices and research center in Manhattan, the patents and copyrights...

It's later and the shadows are long. Ally and Lyman are seated at the island. Ally is staring at a point inside herself.

Elmo lets himself in. He throws his guitar and jacket on the sofa and starts down the hallway. Janice comes out of the bedroom pulling on a robe.

Tears track Elmo's cheeks.

Elmo is still crying. He doesn't seem to notice that all the lights for ten blocks ahead are flashing red.

The Studebaker whistles through a red light.

We can SEE the red wash over Elmo's face as he rushes under one, two, three, four lights. A car coming along a side street suddenly jumps into the corner of OUR EYE. Elmo doesn't see it until it fills the windshield in front of him. Instinctively, he throws the wheel to the left.

The other driver counters by swerving to the right. In a cloud of burnt rubber, the two cars miss. Elmo's skid stops against the curb with the Studebaker now facing the direction it came from. The other car keeps going.

Ally and Lyman are in the back seat. The MUSIC GOES INTO AN INSTRUMENTAL PHASE.

Yes.

The musicians are in their cubicles. A chorus of friends and lovers is around a microphone. The Singer is in front of another one. The song pumps to the end and then it's over. And they believed in me.

The TWO SETS SPLIT INTO FOUR adding more INTERVIEWERS. The FOUR INTO EIGHT, the EIGHT INTO SIXTEEN until the SCREEN IS FILLED WITH INTERVIEWERS interviewing Elmo. The babble of questions and answers continues until it is overpowered by a large group of ragged VOICES SINGING "HAPPY BIRTHDAY". They continue through:
EXT. NEW YORK SIDEWALK - DAY

Elmo and Brian walk up to a crowded corner of an intersection. While they wait for the light to change, we SEE that Ally is only one person ahead of Elmo. The light changes and almost together Elmo and Ally walk across the street. When they reach the other side, Elmo turns left and Ally goes right.
INT. FOUR SEASONS RESTAURANT - NEW YORK - AFTERNOON

The record Executives standing at their places around a banquet table finish their song. EXECUTIVES ... happy birthday, dear Elmo, happy Elmo, seated behind a record-shaped cake with "Happy 30th" written on it, shyly acknowledges their applause. Elmo looks up at Brian, who is standing next to him.
INT. BLOOMINGDALE'S - NEW YORK - AFTERNOON

Ally is browsing at one of the cosmetic counters. She sprays a scent on her hand and, as she raises it to her nose, she sees Tom turn away from another cosmetic counter on the other side of the aisle. Maybe he feels her eyes on him, but for some reason he looks over and sees her. For a beat they stare at each other and then Tom crosses the aisle to her.
Hi.
Hi.
How are you?
Fine. How about you?
Good. I hear you're running the company.
I'm trying to.
You look great.
Ally is embarrassed.
I haven't done my feature yet. I'm still doing commercials.
You will.
A petite BLONDE, more of a girl than a woman, slips up and attaches herself to Tom.
Oh, eh... Ally, I'd like you to meet my wife. Pam, this is Ally.
Oh. Hello, Ally.
Hello.
We better go, Tom, we're gonna be late.
Let's have lunch some time; I'll give you a call.
Okay...
Nice meeting you.
Ally nods and we WATCH the sadness fill her face as she watches them go.
EXT. FOUR SEASONS - LATE AFTERNOON
It's RAINING. A limousine is waiting for Elmo and Brian as they come out of the restaurant. They're about to get into it when Elmo stops.
I think I'll walk.
It's raining.
Yeah... I just feel like I want to walk.
(getting into the car) Okay, I'll see you back at the hotel.
(stops the closing door) Hey, Brian... is this real?
It's real.
INT. CHANDLER TOY CO. - LATE AFTERNOON
Ally comes out of her office. She's troubled.
(to secretary) I'm leaving, Candy.
Lyman called while you were on the
phone.
I'll call him later.
INT. ELEVATOR - LATE AFTERNOON
Ally is all alone. She is leaning against the back wall, thinking.
EXT. STREET - LATE AFTERNOON
It's RAINING. There is a limo waiting at the curb. When the CHAUFFEUR sees Ally coming, he moves to open the door.
I'm gonna walk tonight, Bill. I'll see you in the morning.
EXT. STREET

It's STILL RAINING. Elmo is smiling as he wends his way along the sidewalk. His MUSIC IS EVERYWHERE. A GHETTO BLASTER is PLAYING IT. It's coming from a shop front; from a passing CAR. It RICOCHETS around him like echoes in a canyon.
EXT. STREET
The music is all around Ally but she's sad.
EXT. STREET

Elmo is halfway through the intersection when it happens. A car runs the light and hits him. He is thrown up on the sidewalk and he lays there very still. His eyes are closed. As we MOVE IN ON his face, the SCREEN GETS DARKER AND DARKER UNTIL IT'S BLACK. And then the BLACKNESS BEGINS TO MOVE. SLOW AT FIRST, THEN FASTER AND FASTER. As we REACH MAXIMUM VELOCITY, CHUNKS OF LIGHT HURTLE out of the darkness AT US. Suddenly, we're IN A TUNNEL OF COLORED WHITE LIGHT, going EVEN FASTER than we were in the darkness. And then we STOP. In a WHITE ROOM. Elmo is standing in the middle of it. And he's naked.
Hello, Elmo.
Elmo turns around. Emmett, in his blue coveralls and flannel shirt, is coming toward him.
Where am I? What happened?
You were hit by a car. But you're gonna be all right. She found you.
I don't understand. What do you mean?
I love you, Elmo.
INT. HOSPITAL ROOM - EVENING

The "ELVIRA MADIGAN" THEME FADES IN. Ally is sitting in the shadows at the foot of the bed watching Elmo sleep. It's as if she's trying to remember where she saw his face before. After a long time she gets up and brushes a damp curl off his forehead. She goes to the window and stares out. The rain has stopped. In the last light of day, a rainbow arcs over the city. She sighs and goes to the edge of the bed. Elmo is still sleeping peacefully. She takes his hand. Life is what happens while you're making other plans. THE END
